annotations.label: Slide rule

anchor.snippet: A slide rule is a hand-operated mechanical calculator consisting of slidable rulers

annotations.note: A physical mechanical-calculator device, not a mathematical object Mathlib formalizes.

annotations.label: Scales as lookup tables

anchor.snippet: Each ruler's scale has graduations labeled with precomputed outputs of various mathematical functions

annotations.note: Describes engraved physical graduations of function values, with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Multiplication by summing logarithms

anchor.snippet: a number to be multiplied on one logarithmic-scale ruler can be aligned with the start of another such ruler to sum their logarithms

annotations.note: The principle that adding logarithm positions multiplies the numbers is exactly log (x*y) = log x + log y (Real.log_mul).

annotations.label: Parts of a slide rule

anchor.snippet: Most slide rules consist of three parts

annotations.note: A physical component description (stock, slide, cursor) with no mathematical formalization.

annotations.label: Decade

anchor.snippet: Scales may be grouped in decades , where each decade corresponds to a range of numbers that spans a ratio of 10

annotations.note: A naming convention for a factor-of-ten scale range, not a formalized definition.

annotations.label: Logarithmic identities for multiplication and division

anchor.snippet: The following logarithmic identities transform the operations of multiplication and division to addition and subtraction

annotations.note: These are exactly Real.log_mul (log(x*y)=log x+log y) and Real.log_div (log(x/y)=log x-log y) in the same module.

annotations.label: Multiplication procedure

anchor.snippet: corresponds to shifting the top logarithmic scale by a distance of

annotations.note: The shifting procedure relies on Real.log_mul, but the physical sliding operation itself is not a formalized statement.

annotations.label: Multiplication 3×2=6

anchor.snippet: the result of the multiplication 3×2=6 can then be read on the bottom scale under the top scale's

annotations.note: A concrete arithmetic example (provable by norm_num) with no dedicated named declaration.

annotations.label: Multiplication 7×2=14

anchor.snippet: the answer to 7×2=14 is found by first positioning the top scale to start above the 2 of the bottom scale

annotations.note: A concrete arithmetic worked example with no specific Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Multiplication 88×20

anchor.snippet: to multiply 88×20 , the top scale is again positioned to start at the 2 on the bottom scale

annotations.note: A concrete arithmetic worked example with no specific Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: General multiplication rule

anchor.snippet: In general, the 1 on the top is moved to a factor on the bottom, and the answer is read off the bottom where the other factor is on the top

annotations.note: The underlying additivity of logarithms is Real.log_mul, but the slide-rule positioning procedure is not formalized.

annotations.label: Division 5.5/2

anchor.snippet: The 2 on the top scale is placed over the 5.5 on the bottom scale

annotations.note: A concrete division worked example with no specific Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Alternating multiplication and division

anchor.snippet: movement of the scales can be minimized by alternating divisions and multiplications

annotations.note: A practical procedural optimization for slide-rule use, not a mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Aligned numbers form constant ratios

anchor.snippet: pairs of numbers that are aligned on the logarithmic scales form constant ratios

annotations.note: Constant offset in log positions equals constant ratio follows from Real.log_div, but the proportion statement is not formalized directly.

annotations.label: Solving proportions example

anchor.snippet: setting 7.5 on one scale over 10 on the other scale

annotations.note: A concrete proportion worked example with no specific Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Auxiliary scales

anchor.snippet: some slide rules have other mathematical functions encoded on other auxiliary scales

annotations.note: Describes physical engraving of extra function scales, with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Squaring via D and A scales

anchor.snippet: locate x on the D scale and read its square on the A scale

annotations.note: The A-vs-D squaring relation rests on log(x^n)=n*log x (Real.log_pow), but the scale-reading procedure is not formalized.

annotations.label: Powers via LL scales

anchor.snippet: When several LL scales are present, use the one with x on it

annotations.note: LL-scale exponentiation depends on log(x^y)=y*log x (Real.log_rpow), but the multi-scale procedure is not a formalized statement.

annotations.label: Cube root extraction

anchor.snippet: To extract a cube root using a slide rule with only C/D and A/B scales

annotations.note: The cube-root technique relies on logarithm-power scaling (Real.log_rpow), but the manual extraction procedure is not formalized.

annotations.label: Roots of quadratic equations

anchor.snippet: Quadratic equations of the form

annotations.note: The roots of a*x^2+b*x+c=0 are characterized exactly by quadratic_eq_zero_iff via the discriminant.

annotations.label: Future value via LLN scales

anchor.snippet: The LLN scales can be used to compute and compare the cost or return on a fixed rate loan or investment

annotations.note: The compound-interest/future-value finance application is not formalized in Mathlib (only the underlying power function exists).

annotations.label: Trig scales S, T, ST

anchor.snippet: The S, T, and ST scales are used for trig functions and multiples of trig functions

annotations.note: The trig functions themselves (Real.sin, Real.tan) are defined, but the S/T/ST scale encodings are device features, not formalized.

annotations.label: Computing sines

anchor.snippet: sines are found by comparing the S scale with C (or D) scale

annotations.note: Real.sin is fully formalized, but the scale-comparison procedure for reading sines is not.

annotations.label: Computing tangents

anchor.snippet: Tangents are found by comparing the T scale with the C (or D) scale for angles less than 45 degrees

annotations.note: Real.tan is formalized, but the T-vs-C scale-reading procedure for tangents is not.

annotations.label: Small-angle approximation

anchor.snippet: For angles below 5.7 degrees, sines, tangents, and radians are approximately equal

annotations.note: Mathlib has the bounds Real.sin_lt (sin x < x) and Real.lt_tan (x < tan x), but not the informal small-angle 'approximately equal' statement itself.

annotations.label: Base-10 logarithms via L scale

anchor.snippet: Base-10 logarithms and exponentials are found using the L scale, which is linear

annotations.note: Base-10 logarithm is the special case Real.logb 10, but the linear L-scale reading procedure is not formalized.

annotations.label: Logarithms to any base

anchor.snippet: Logarithms to any other base can be calculated by reversing the procedure for calculating powers of a number

annotations.note: Arbitrary-base logarithm is Real.logb (log x / log b), and its inverse-of-power character is Real.rpow_logb/Real.logb_rpow.

annotations.label: Addition/subtraction via division identity

anchor.snippet: Exploits the identity that the quotient of two variables plus (or minus) one times the divisor equals their sum (or difference)

annotations.note: The identity (a/b + 1)·b = a + b is div_add_one (a/b+1=(a+b)/b), with the subtractive form div_sub_one.

anchors.snippet: Exploits the identity that the quotient of two variables plus (or minus) one times the divisor equals their sum (or difference)

annotations.label: Addition/subtraction via linear L scale

anchor.snippet: After sliding the cursor right (for addition) or left (for subtraction) and returning the slide to 0, the result can be read

annotations.note: A purely mechanical cursor-sliding procedure on a linear scale, not a formalized mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Calculations with monotonic scales

anchor.snippet: Using (almost) any strictly monotonic scales , other calculations can also be made with one movement

annotations.note: The notion of strictly monotonic functions (StrictMono) is formalized, but the slide-rule generalization claim is not a Mathlib theorem.

anchors.snippet: Using (almost) any strictly monotonic scales , other calculations can also be made with one movement

annotations.label: Quadratic scales (Pythagorean)

anchor.snippet: quadratic scales can be used to solve

annotations.note: The √(x²±y²) Pythagorean computation uses Real.sqrt, which is formalized, but the quadratic-scale construction itself is not.

anchors.snippet: quadratic scales can be used to solve

annotations.label: Reciprocal scales

anchor.snippet: Reciprocal scales can be used for the equality

annotations.note: Although reciprocal/inverse operations exist in Mathlib, the unspecified reciprocal-scale equality is a device feature with no matching formalized statement.

anchors.snippet: Reciprocal scales can be used for the equality

annotations.label: Order of magnitude example

anchor.snippet: 1.5 × 30 (which equals 45) will show the same result as 1 500 000 × 0.03

annotations.note: An observation that slide rules ignore decimal magnitude (mantissa identity), illustrative rather than a formalized theorem.
